1: ʾăbārĕkâ ʾet-YHWH bĕkol-ʿēt tāmîd tĕhillātô bĕpî (ʾăḇāreḵâ ʾeṯ-YHWH bĕḵol-ʿēṯ tāmîḏ tĕhillāṯô ḇĕp̄î): 'I will bless the LORD at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth.' Psalm 34 is an acrostic poem (each verse beginning with successive Hebrew letters, ʾālep through tāw), attributed to David's feigning of madness before Achish king of Gath (1 Samuel 21:10-15 — the superscription uses 'Abimelech,' possibly a dynastic title for Philistine kings). The cohortative ʾăbārĕkâ ('I will bless') opens with resolve: tāmîd ('continually, always') negates any temporal limitation on praise. The phrase tĕhillātô bĕpî, 'his praise in my mouth,' locates worship in the mouth — the organ of both speech and ingestion, connecting to the later 'taste and see' of Psalms 34:8.
2: baYHWH tithallēl napšî (ḇaYHWH tiṯhallēl napšî): 'My soul makes its boast in the LORD.' The hitpael tiṯhallēl ('boasts, glories') from hālal is reflexive — the soul's self-glorying redirected toward YHWH. The Pauline principle of 1 Corinthians 1:31 ('let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord') draws on this Psalter tradition (cf. Jeremiah 9:23-24). The phrase yišmĕʿû ʿănāwîm wĕyiśmāḥû, 'the humble will hear and be glad,' introduces the ʿănāwîm as those who listen to testimony of rescue and share vicariously in the joy — the community of the poor who live by hearing good news of others' deliverance.
3: gaddĕlû laYHWH ʾittî ûnĕrômĕmâ šĕmô yaḥdāw (ḡaddĕlû laYHWH ʾittî ûnĕrômĕmâ šĕmô yaḥdāw): 'Oh, magnify the LORD with me, and let us exalt his name together!' The plural imperatives gaddĕlû ('make great!') and nĕrômĕmâ ('let us exalt!') invite communal amplification of praise. The adverb yaḥdāw ('together') is covenantal — individual rescue becomes corporate celebration. The 'name' (šēm) that is exalted encompasses YHWH's revealed character: the rescue of David (background of the psalm) becomes a public declaration about who YHWH is.
4: dāraštî ʾet-YHWH wĕʿānānî ûmikkol-mĕgûrôtay hiṣṣîlānî (ḏāraštî ʾeṯ-YHWH wĕʿānānî ûmikkol-mĕḡûrôṯay hiṣṣîlānî): 'I sought the LORD, and he answered me and delivered me from all my fears.' The verb dāraš ('to seek, inquire after') is the psalmist's primary act — the seeking that initiated the entire encounter. The reciprocal wĕʿānānî ('and he answered me') shows that seeking activates divine response. The noun mĕgûrôt ('fears, terrors') from gûr ('to sojourn, dread') is unusual — it may connote the alien anxiety of someone in a foreign context (David among Philistines), making the historical backstory theologically loaded: fear of exile rescued by YHWH.
5: hibbîṭû ʾēlāyw wĕnāhārû ûpĕnêhem ʾal-yēḥpārû (hibbîṭû ʾēlāyw wĕnāhārû ûp̄ĕnêhem ʾal-yēḥp̄ārû): 'Those who looked to him were radiant, and their faces shall never be ashamed.' The plural hibbîṭû ('they looked') shifts from David's individual experience to the community's: looking toward YHWH has a visible effect — wĕnāhārû ('they were radiant'), from nāhar ('to shine, stream, be radiant'). The shamelessness of their faces — ûpĕnêhem ʾal-yēḥpārû — echoes the Psalter's consistent prayer (e.g., Psalms 25:2-3) that those who trust YHWH will not be put to shame. The inversion: looking toward YHWH produces light; looking away produces shame.
6: zeh ʿānî qārāʾ wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôtāyw hôšîʿô (zeh ʿānî qārāʾ wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôṯāyw hôšîʿô): 'This poor man cried, and the LORD heard him and saved him out of all his troubles.' The demonstrative zeh ʿānî ('this poor man') is emphatic and personal — David identifies himself as the paradigmatic ʿānî ('afflicted/poor one'). The sequence qārāʾ → šāmaʿ → hôšîʿ ('cried → heard → saved') is the compressed rescue narrative of the entire Psalter. The psalm is not just testimony about YHWH's past action but a pattern: ʿānî + cry → YHWH hears → rescue. Peter applies this pattern to Jesus (1 Peter 5:7).
7: ḥōneh malʾak YHWH sābîb lîrēʾāyw wayyĕḥallĕṣēm (ḥōneh malʾak YHWH sāḇîḇ lîrēʾāyw wayyĕḥallĕṣēm): 'The angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear him and delivers them.' The participle ḥōneh ('encamps, sets up camp') from ḥānâ applies military encampment language to divine protection: the divine envoy establishes a perimeter. The malʾak YHWH ('messenger/angel of the LORD') in the OT is often indistinguishable from YHWH himself (Genesis 16:7-13; Psalms 22:11-18; Exodus 3:2-6) — an anticipation of the Second Person's mediatorial role. The scope: lîrēʾāyw ('for those who fear him') — the protective encampment is covenant-shaped, not universal. The hiphil wayyĕḥallĕṣēm ('and he delivers them') from ḥālaṣ ('to draw out') describes rescue as extraction from danger.
8: ṭaʿămû ûrĕʾû kî ṭôb YHWH (ṭaʿămû ûrĕʾû kî ṭôḇ YHWH): 'Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good.' The imperatives ṭaʿămû ('taste!') and rĕʾû ('see!') are synesthetic — taste and sight combined invite experiential rather than merely intellectual knowledge of divine goodness. The verb ṭāʿam ('to taste, perceive by eating') makes covenant knowledge bodily: one does not merely learn about YHWH's goodness but experiences it as real as food in the mouth. 1 Peter 2:3 cites this verse: 'if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good' — applied to the community's experience of Christ. The following beatitude ʾašrê haggeber yeḥĕseh-bô, 'blessed is the man who takes refuge in him,' links tasting to ḥāsâ-trust.
9: yĕrĕʾû ʾet-YHWH qĕdōšāyw kî ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw (yĕrĕʾû ʾeṯ-YHWH qĕḏôšāyw kî ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw): 'Oh, fear the LORD, you his holy ones, for those who fear him have no lack!' The imperative yĕrĕʾû is directed at qĕdōšāyw ('his holy ones, his consecrated ones') — the covenant community already set apart for YHWH. Fear here (yārēʾ) is not terror but the reverence that orientates all of life toward YHWH. The promise: ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw, 'no lack to those who fear him' — the noun maḥsôr ('want, lack, deficiency') is negated absolutely. Those who fear YHWH have no permanent deficiency; the root of all their needs is met in him.
10: kĕpîrîm rāšû wĕrāʿēbû wĕdōrĕšê YHWH lōʾ-yaḥsĕrû kol-ṭôb (kĕp̄îrîm rāšû wĕrāʿēḇû wĕḏōrĕšê YHWH lōʾ-yaḥsĕrû ḵol-ṭôḇ): 'The young lions suffer want and hunger, but those who seek the LORD lack no good thing.' The contrast is between kĕpîrîm ('young lions' — the most powerful predators in their prime strength) and dōrĕšê YHWH ('those who seek the LORD'). Young lions — paragons of physical power and hunting ability — still experience rāšû ('become poor') and rāʿēbû ('hunger'). But the YHWH-seekers yaḥsĕrû... kol-ṭôb ('lack no good thing'). Power and provision are not correlated — seeking YHWH, not personal strength, is the ground of sufficiency.
11: lĕkû bānîm šimʿû-lî yirʾat YHWH ʾălammēdkem (lĕḵû ḇānîm šimʿû-lî yirʾaṯ YHWH ʾălammēḏkem): 'Come, O children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the LORD.' The shift to wisdom-address — lĕkû bānîm ('come, children') — transforms the psalm from personal testimony to pedagogical discourse: David's rescue becomes a teaching moment. The phrase yirʾat YHWH ʾălammēdkem, 'I will teach you the fear of the LORD,' identifies the fear of YHWH as teachable — a disposition cultivated by instruction and practice, not merely intuited. This verse inaugurates the sapiential center of the psalm (Psalms 34:11-14).
12: mî-hāʾîš heḥāpēṣ ḥayyîm ʾōhēb yāmîm lirʾôt ṭôb (mî-hāʾîš heḥāpēṣ ḥayyîm ʾōhēb yāmîm lirʾôṯ ṭôḇ): 'What man is there who desires life and loves many days, that he may see good?' The rhetorical question mî-hāʾîš ('what man?') creates a universally appealing hook: who does not desire life and good days? The two participles heḥāpēṣ ḥayyîm ('who desires life') and ʾōhēb yāmîm ('who loves days') are universal — the question's answer is everyone. The condition 'to see good' (lirʾôt ṭôb) picks up the 'see' of Psalms 34:8's synesthetic invitation: seeing good is possible, and the means is covenant wisdom.
13: nĕṣōr lĕšônĕkā mērāʿ ûśĕpātêkā middabbēr mirmâ (nĕṣōr lĕšôneḵā mērāʿ ûśĕp̄āṯeḵā middabbēr mirmâ): 'Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from speaking deceit.' The verb nāṣar (qal imperative, 'guard, keep watch') — a military term — applied to the tongue describes speech as a territory to be defended. The contrast between ṭôb ('good') and rāʿ ('evil'), ʾĕmet ('truth') and mirmâ ('deceit'), frames the tongue as the battleground of covenantal integrity. Peter quotes Psalms 34:12-16 extensively in 1 Peter 3:10-12, applying the ethical instruction of this psalm to the Christian community's social witness — the passage from Psalter wisdom becomes apostolic exhortation.
14: sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôb baqqēš šālôm wĕrādĕpēhû (sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôb baqqēš šālôm wĕrāḏĕp̄ēhû): 'Turn away from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it.' The four imperatives create a complete ethical program: sûr ('turn away') + waʿăśēh ('do') + baqqēš ('seek') + wĕrādĕpēhû ('pursue'). The verb rādap ('pursue') — normally of enemies chasing someone (cf. Psalms 7:1) — is applied to šālôm ('peace'): peace must be actively chased, not passively hoped for. The movement from avoidance (sûr mērāʿ) to action (ʿăśēh ṭôb) to pursuit (rādap šālôm) describes the full range of ethical life, corresponding to the OT/NT summary of avoiding evil and doing good (Romans 12:9; 1 Peter 3:11).
15: ʿênê YHWH ʾel-ṣaddîqîm wĕʾoznāyw ʾel-šawʿātām (ʿênê YHWH ʾel-ṣaddîqîm wĕʾoznāyw ʾel-šawʿāṯām): 'The eyes of the LORD are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry.' The double divine attention — ʿênê YHWH ('eyes of the LORD') and ʾoznāyw ('his ears') — ensures complete sensory coverage: YHWH both sees the righteous and hears their cry. Peter cites this verse in 1 Peter 3:12 as the theological ground for the ethical instruction of Psalms 34:13-14: righteous conduct is worth maintaining because YHWH is watching and listening. The noun šawʿâ ('cry for help') is the desperate outcry of those in extremity — the same type of cry that initiates rescue throughout the Psalter.
16: pĕnê YHWH bĕʿōśê rāʿ lĕhakrît mēʾereṣ zikrām (p̄ĕnê YHWH bĕʿōśê rāʿ lĕhaḵrîṯ mēʾereṣ zikrām): 'The face of the LORD is against those who do evil, to cut off the memory of them from the earth.' The contrast with Psalms 34:15 is structurally intentional: YHWH's face (pānîm) is oriented toward the righteous beneficially (Psalms 34:15) but toward evildoers destructively. The phrase lĕhakrît mēʾereṣ zikrām, 'to cut off their memory from the earth,' invokes the covenant curse of obliterated remembrance (cf. Psalms 9:5; Deuteronomy 9:14). Memory-erasure is the final form of judgment: not just death but the annihilation of legacy. Peter also quotes this verse, bracketing the moral teaching with divine scrutiny from both sides.
17: ṣāʿăqû wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôtām hiṣṣîlām (ṣāʿăqû wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôṯām hiṣṣîlām): 'When the righteous cry for help, the LORD hears and delivers them out of all their troubles.' The sequence ṣāʿăqû → šāmaʿ → hiṣṣîlām ('cried → heard → delivered') repeats the pattern of Psalms 34:6 with generalizing third-person plurals: what happened to David (Psalms 34:6) is paradigmatic for all the righteous. The noun ṣārôt ('troubles, straits') encompasses every form of distress — and the promise applies to mikkol ('out of all'), without exception. The righteousness that attracts YHWH's hearing is the covenant loyalty described in Psalms 34:13-14, not moral perfection.
18: qārôb YHWH lĕnišbĕrê-lēb wĕʾet-dakkĕʾê rûaḥ yôšîaʿ (qārôḇ YHWH lĕnišbĕrê-lēḇ wĕʾeṯ-daḵḵĕʾê rûaḥ yôšîaʿ): 'The LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit.' The two recipients — nišbĕrê-lēb ('broken of heart, shattered in heart') and dakkĕʾê rûaḥ ('crushed of spirit') — describe the person who has experienced catastrophic loss of inner resilience. The adjective dakkāʾ ('crushed, pulverized') from dakāʾ implies being ground down to powder — not merely injured but disintegrated. To such persons YHWH is qārôb ('near') — not distant and requiring approach but already proximate. Jesus's beatitude 'blessed are the poor in spirit' (Matthew 5:3) and Isaiah 61:1's mission to 'bind up the brokenhearted' both draw on this verse's theology.
19: rabbôt rāʿôt ṣaddîq ûmikkullām yaṣṣîlennû YHWH (rabbôṯ rāʿôṯ ṣaddîq ûmikkulām yaṣṣîlennû YHWH): 'Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the LORD delivers him out of them all.' The phrase rabbôt rāʿôt ṣaddîq ('many are the troubles of the righteous') is not a complaint but a description — righteous people do suffer, and much. The psalm refuses the false theology that righteousness guarantees comfort. But the counter: ûmikkullām yaṣṣîlennû YHWH ('from all of them the LORD delivers him') — the word kullām ('all of them') makes the rescue scope absolute. The righteous suffer much AND are delivered completely. Both are true; neither cancels the other (cf. 2 Corinthians 4:8-9; Acts 14:22).
20: šōmēr kol-ʿaṣmôtāyw ʾaḥat mēhēnnâ lōʾ nišbārâ (šōmēr ḵol-ʿaṣmôṯāyw ʾaḥaṯ mēhēnnâ lōʾ nišbārâ): 'He keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken.' The verb šāmar ('guard, keep') applied to ʿaṣmôt ('bones') in the context of Psalms 34:19's 'many afflictions' — YHWH protects even the skeletal structure of the righteous through suffering. John 19:36 explicitly cites this verse as fulfilled at the crucifixion: the soldiers did not break Jesus's legs (as was customary to hasten death) because he was already dead — John sees in this the fulfillment of Psalms 34:20 (and Exodus 12:46, the Passover bone-protection). The most literal fulfillment of a Psalter verse in the NT passion narrative.
21: tĕmôtēt rāšāʿ rāʿâ wĕśōnĕʾê ṣaddîq yĕʾšāmû (ṯĕmôṯēṯ rāšāʿ rāʿâ wĕśōnĕʾê ṣaddîq yĕʾšāmû): 'Affliction will slay the wicked, and those who hate the righteous will be condemned.' The verb mātat (hiphil, 'to cause to die') applied to the rāʿâ ('evil') that kills the rāšāʿ ('wicked') is an ironic reversal: evil, which the wicked used against others, destroys them. The phrase śōnĕʾê ṣaddîq yĕʾšāmû, 'those who hate the righteous will be condemned/guilty,' uses the niphal ʾāšam ('to become guilty, bear guilt') — the haters of the righteous are found guilty before YHWH's court. The reversal of Psalms 34:19-20 continues: the righteous suffer but are preserved; the wicked triumph but are finally destroyed by their own evil.
22: pôdeh YHWH nepeš ʿăbādāyw wĕlōʾ yĕʾšĕmû kol-haḥōsîm bô (p̄ôḏeh YHWH nepeš ʿăḇāḏāyw wĕlōʾ yĕʾšĕmû ḵol-haḥōsîm ḇô): 'The LORD redeems the life of his servants; none of those who take refuge in him will be condemned.' The participle pôdeh ('redeems') from pādâ ('to ransom, redeem') in present tense indicates ongoing activity: YHWH continuously ransoms. The object nepeš ʿăbādāyw ('the life of his servants') encompasses the whole person. The final promise — lōʾ yĕʾšĕmû kol-haḥōsîm bô, 'none who take refuge in him will be condemned' — returns to the ḥāsâ root of Psalms 34:8 and answers Psalms 34:21's condemnation of the wicked: the condemned are those who hate the righteous; those who take refuge are never condemned. The acrostic closes with an absolute guarantee of non-condemnation for the covenant community (cf. Romans 8:1).
